coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen peroxide enhanced glow-type chemiluminescence of hydrazine hydrate modified carbon quantum dots-potassium persulfate system
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Shu-Hua
Huang, Dan-Dan
Cheng, Zhang-Jian
Liu, Ai-Lin
Lei, Yun
coredata.dc:description: Most known chemiluminescence (CL) systems are flash-type that generate weak luminescence and decline quickly after dozens of seconds, while the glow-type CL systems have stable emission for an extended period to achieve accurate quantitation. In this work, a long-term CL system based on hydrazine-hydrate (N2H4·H2O) modified carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) as a luminescent probe, with K2S2O8 and H2O2 as co-reactants, was proposed. The CL emission enhanced by H2O2 increased 18-fold more than that of N-CQDs and K2S2O8 direct reaction, and decayed by 5% of the maximum intensity over 700 s. In the reaction system, K2S2O8 and H2O2 co-reactants can promote each other to continuously generate corresponding radicals (•OH, O2 •−, 1O2), which in turn trigger the CL emission of N-CQDs. This phenomenon was identified as the primary cause for the production of persistent CL. In addition, a stable and selective CL sensor based on the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 CL enhancing system was developed for ascorbic acid quantitation in the linear range from 0.1 to 10.0 mM with a detection limit of 0.036 mM. The method has been applied to the analysis of tablet samples and holds potential in pharmaceutical analysis field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrazine hydrate reduction
Carbon quantum dots
Glow-type chemiluminescence
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CL mechanism of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system
N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 based CL sensor for AA detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of N-CQDs and the CL mechanism of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system.

(A) TEM image (inset: size distributions of N-CQDs) and (B) HRTEM image of N-CQDs. XPS survey spectra (C) and FT-IR spectra (F) of CQDs and N-CQDs; well-fitted C1s XPS spectra of CQDs (D) and N-CQDs (E) and well-fitted N1s XPS spectra of CQDs (G) and N-CQDs (H).

Kinetic curves of different systems (A). Kinetic curve of the CL reaction of 5 μ g/mL N-CQDs with 0.1 M K2S2O8 and 5ⅹ105 μM H2O2 (B). Effect of CQDs and N2H4·H2O on the CL intensity (C). Effect of sampling sequence on the CL intensity of N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system (D).

ESR analysis of N-CQDs-K2S2O8 system (a, black curves) and N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system (b, red curves). The ESR spectra of DMPO-•OH adduct at 2 min (A) and 5 min (B). The ESR spectra of DMPO-O2 •− adduct at 2 min (C) and 5 min (D). The ESR spectra of TEMPO- 1O2 adduct at 2 min (E) and 5 min (F), respectively. Reaction conditions: 5 μg/mL N-CQDs, 0.1 M K2S2O8, 5ⅹ105 μM H2O2, and 0.1 M PBS (pH = 12). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) Calibration curve for the detection of AA in N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system, and (B) effect of coexistent substances on the detection of N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 for AA.
Relative content (%) of different functional groups on the surface of CQDs and N-CQDs (calculated by integrating fitting curve area of C1s XPS and N1s XPS).
Determination of AA in real samples.

head.ce:title: Hydrogen peroxide enhanced glow-type chemiluminescence of hydrazine hydrate modified carbon quantum dots-potassium persulfate system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province, The School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province, The School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Most known chemiluminescence (CL) systems are flash-type that generate weak luminescence and decline quickly after dozens of seconds, while the glow-type CL systems have stable emission for an extended period to achieve accurate quantitation. In this work, a long-term CL system based on hydrazine-hydrate (N2H4·H2O) modified carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) as a luminescent probe, with K2S2O8 and H2O2 as co-reactants, was proposed. The CL emission enhanced by H2O2 increased 18-fold more than that of N-CQDs and K2S2O8 direct reaction, and decayed by 5% of the maximum intensity over 700 s. In the reaction system, K2S2O8 and H2O2 co-reactants can promote each other to continuously generate corresponding radicals (•OH, O2 •−, 1O2), which in turn trigger the CL emission of N-CQDs. This phenomenon was identified as the primary cause for the production of persistent CL. In addition, a stable and selective CL sensor based on the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 CL enhancing system was developed for ascorbic acid quantitation in the linear range from 0.1 to 10.0 mM with a detection limit of 0.036 mM. The method has been applied to the analysis of tablet samples and holds potential in pharmaceutical analysis field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) refers to a kind of emissive light through chemical reactions involving electronically excited intermediates or products which subsequently relax into the ground state by releasing energy with light emission [1,2]. CL has gained increasing popularity in various fields such as pharmaceutical analysis, clinical research, biotechnology, environmental monitoring and food analysis due to its inherent advantages, including the absent of an external light source, simple instrumentation, easy operation and quick detection [3,4]. Typically, it is commonly observed that chemiluminescence systems, such as luminol-H2O2, luminol-KIO4 and lucigenin-H2O2, exhibit transient chemiluminescence and rapidly decline within dozens of seconds. This characteristic often leads to poor analysis accuracy and reproducibility, thereby impeding their practical applications [5].

In contrast, the glow-type CL has stable emission for an extended period, which is more conducive to the accurate quantitation and imaging analysis [6–9]. In 2017, Liu et al. prepared a CL hydrogel for a long-lasting CL emission, which was so intense that could be observed with naked eyes in a dark room [10]. Xie et al. proposed a long-life CL system, which was composed of sodium alginate source hydrogels, the traditional CL reagent isoluminol and a catalyst Co2+ [11]. Li’s group also used the traditional CL reagent luminol, and developed a catalyst-free co-reaction luminol-H2O2-K2S2O8 CL system, showing long-lasting and intense CL emission [12]. In addition, researchers found that some nanomaterials could induce long-lasting CL emission [13–16]. Li’s group developed a long-persistent CL imaging system of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/H2O2 using Cu2+ ion as a catalyst [14]. The mimic-enzyme catalytic polystyrene-hemin dots was proposed as a nanoprobe for CL imaging [15]. A persistent CL system was developed by the local tandem catalysis of substrates via nano-/bio-enzymes consisting of iron porphyrin metal–organic frameworks and glucose oxidase [8]. However, the constructions of these long-lasting CL systems invariably involved the use of porous materials for loading CL reagent or the need of extra catalysts, thereby entailing a complicated preparation process. Consequently, developing a straightforward and catalyst-free long-lasting CL reaction process assumes paramount importance.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm [17], have attracted extensive attention from researchers since first obtained by Xu et al. in 2004 [18]. These eco-friendly nanomaterials with unique luminescent properties have found widespread applications in sensing, photocatalysis and other fields due to their facile preparation, abundant functional groups on their surface, as well as outstanding electronic properties. According to the existing research reports, CQDs serve as emitting species, energy receptors and catalysts in CL reactions, which can effectively enhance the sensitivity and stability of CL [19–21]. For example, Zhang et al. reported that the CL intensity of CQDs-K2S2O8 could be extraordinary enhanced in the presence of triethylamine [22]. Shah et al. observed an enhancement in the CL signals of the KIO4-H2O2 system in basic medium by nitrogen doped carbon dots [23]. However, it is worth noting that all of these CL systems exhibit flash-type light emission.

Ascorbic acid (AA), also recognized as vitamin C, is an indispensable micronutrient and a potent antioxidant that neutralizes free radicals. An imbalance in AA consumption, either insufficient or excessive, can precipitate diseases such as scurvy, stomach spasms, and other chronic diseases [24]. Up to now, different analytical methods have been employed for the quantification of AA, including ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra, fluorescence, chromatography, electrochemical techniques and chemiluminescence [25–30]. In this study, a glow-type CL system is developed by utilizing carbon quantum dots as luminophore to achieve superior CL performance. As shown in Fig. 1 , pristine CQDs are first synthesized through nitric acid oxidation of carbon fiber, and then reduced by hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O) to obtain N2H4·H2O modified carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). Remarkably, N-CQDs can exhibit persistent CL emission at the amid of K2S2O8 and H2O2, whereas pristine CQDs do not. Subsequently, the CL behavior and mechanism of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system are examined. Based on the inhibitory effect of ascorbic acid (AA) on the CL of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system, this approach is employed for the analysis of AA in vitamin C effervescent tablets, yielding satisfactory recoveries.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grades. Ultrapure water obtained from a Milli-Q purification system was used to prepare all aqueous solution. Carbon fiber was purchased from Shanghai Energy Carbon Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O), lipoic acid, folic acid, vitamin E and melatonin were supplied from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), ZnCl2, CaCl2, CuCl2, starch, sucrose, citric acid, magnesium stearate and ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Vitamin C tablets were purchased from Northeast Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Lot number: 5230910, Shenyang, China). 0.1 M phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) were prepared using 0.1 M Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4 and 0.1 M NaCl. 0.1 M K2S2O8 solution was prepared by dissolving K2S2O8 in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4).

The morphology of synthesized CQDs and N-CQDs were evaluated by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV 2600 Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were collected through a FL spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Vertex 70 spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) with KBr pellets in the 4000 − 500 cm−1 region. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with A1 Kα (284.8 eV). The CL intensities were monitored on a model MPI-E electrochemical and chemiluminescence analytical system (Xi’an Remex Electronic Sci. Tech. Co., Ltd., China). Electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements were performed on a JES-FA200 spectrometer (JEOL, Japan).

CQDs were synthesized according to the method reported previously with some modification [31]. Briefly, 0.06 g carbon fiber was cut into small fragment, then added into 60 mL of 7.5 M HNO3 and placed in a three-necked round bottom flask. The reaction vessel was maintained at 130 °C for 48 h. After cooling to room temperature, NaOH was added to the mixture to adjust the pH to neutral. Then, the brownish CQDs suspension were collected after centrifugation (8,000 rpm, 10 min), filtration (0.45 and 0.22 µm Millipore membrane), dialysis (1 kDa molecular weight cutoffs, MWCO), and ultrafiltration (3 kDa MWCO). And then the fraction larger than 3 kDa was further lyophilized to obtain CQDs.

Next, for preparing N-CQDs, 5 M N2H4·H2O was added slowly into equivalent volume 1 mg/mL prepared CQDs. Subsequently the mixture was heated in an oil bath under 95 °C for 24 h and then cooled to room temperature. As a result, N-CQDs were obtained after concentrated by rotary evaporation and dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C.

CL measurements were conducted on an MPI-E CL analyzer at room temperature. In a typical experiment, 285 µL of 0.1 M K2S2O8 was added in a quartz cuvette first, and then 15 µL of 0.1 mg/mL N-CQDs and 15 µL of 0.5 M H2O2 were injected with a microliter syringe from the upper injection port. And the CL emission was measured simultaneously by a photomultiplier tube with a fixed voltage of 750 V.

285 µL of 0.1 M K2S2O8, 15 µL of 0.1 mg/mL N-CQDs and 15 µL of 0.5 M H2O2 and 10 µL of different concentrations of AA solution were sequentially introduced into a quartz cuvette. After a 2-minte incubation period, the CL signals were obtained and recorded with an MPI-E electrochemical and chemiluminescence analytical system.

0.6186 g of vitamin C tablet (100 mg of AA per each tablet) sample was crushed and dissolved in ultrapure water to ensure complete dissolution. The resulting solution was then filtrated with a filter paper, and then transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask and diluted with water (about 5 mg/mL AA). The AA contents in the vitamin C tablet solution was determined by the present CL sensor. Then, the sample was spiked with varying AA standard solutions. The recovery rate was determined by comparing these theoretical concentrations to the experimentally obtained values.

TEM images show that CQDs (Fig. S1A) and N-CQDs (Fig. 2 A) are near spherical and well dispersed. The average diameters of CQDs and N-CQDs were about 2.55 ± 0.07 and 2.56 ± 0.16 nm, respectively (inset of Fig. S1A, Fig. 2A). The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm for CQDs (Fig. S1B) and N-CQDs (Fig. 2B) is attributed to the (100) lattice fringe of graphene [32]. These results indicate the hydrazine hydrate reduction does not have a significant impact on the morphology of the CQDs.

To analysis the surface chemical composite of the samples before and after N2H4·H2O treatment, Fig. 2C compares X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the CQDs and N-CQDs samples. The XPS survey spectra for both samples show three dominant peaks: a C1s peak at 284.2 eV, a N1s peak at 399.7 eV and an O1s peak at 533.7 eV. After hydrazine reduction, the O/C atomic ratio drops from 0.72 to 0.68, indicating that N2H4·H2O reduction restores the electronic conjugation of CQDs [33]. Meanwhile, the N/C atomic ratio increases from 0.04 to 0.11, indicating that CQDs was successfully reduced by N2H4·H2O, with more N elements added to the surface (Table S1). As shown in Fig. 2D and E, the XPS high resolution C1s spectra of CQDs and N-CQDs show four peaks at 284.9, 285.9, 288.2 and 288.9 eV, which are ascribed to the C C/C=C, C−O/C−N, C=N and C=O groups, respectively. In the XPS C1s spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 2D), the calculated relative percentages of the C C/C=C, C O/C−N, C=N and C=O were 50.28%, 22.81%, 11.19% and 15.72%, respectively, whereas the percentages for C−C/C=C, C−O/C−N, C=N and C=O were 45.42%, 21.24%, 19.55%, and 13.79%, respectively, after reduction treatment of N2H4·H2O (Table 1 ). Meanwhile, the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2H) of N-CQDs revealed a soaring content of pyrrolic N/pyrazole N (400.14 eV) (from 25.81% to 75.89%) and the disappearance of oxidized N (406.04 eV) (Table 1). Hence, we speculated that surface oxygen-containing group C=O of pristine CQDs performed Knorr reaction (Fig. S3) during the reduction of N2H4·H2O, resulting in the production of pyrazole N [34]. These results were further corroborated via Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy analysis of surface composition. The FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, as depicted in Fig. 2F, exhibits represented vibrational absorption modes at 3427 cm−1 (stretching vibration of −OH and −NH), 1610 cm−1 (C=O band) and 1326 cm−1 (C−OH). For N-CQDs, a new absorption bands attribute to C−N groups can be observed at 1114 cm−1, indicating that N-CQDs produced new pyrazole N groups structure and existed in a C−N bond due to a possible Knorr reaction with N2H4·H2O. It is evident that the C=O bond of N-CQDs obviously becomes weak compared with CQDs, indicating that the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs were diminished by hydrazine reduction via Knorr reaction, which is consisted with the analyses of the XPS results.

UV–vis and fluorescence (FL) spectra were obtained to provide the evidence of optical characterization of CQDs and N-CQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs and N-CQDs reveal a prominent absorption band at 230 nm, which can be assigned to the π-π* transition of C=C (Fig. S2A). Additionally, the CQDs solution displays a bright yellow emission, while the N-CQDs solution exhibits a bright blue emission under 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. S2B). Moreover, a peak of CQDs at 350 nm was associated with the n-π* transition of C=O, while N-CQDs’ peak at 350 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition of both C=O and C=N. The fluorescence peak of CQDs is located at 529 nm (Fig. S2B, curve b) upon excitation at 468 nm (Fig. S2B, curve a). In contrast, N-CQDs exhibit maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 384 nm (Fig. S2B, curve c) and 458 nm (Fig. S2B, curve d), respectively. The blue-shift in the emission wavelength from 529 to 458 nm was clearly ascribed to the reduction in the carbonyl-group content [35]. Additionally, the fluorescence spectra of CQDs and N-CQDs demonstrate an excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence feature (Fig. S2C and D).

The kinetic study was performed to provide more details that might lead to an explanation of the reaction mechanism. The chemiluminescent property of N-CDQs was investigated by static injection mode. As shown in Fig. 3 A, no CL emissions were found for K2S2O8-H2O2 and N-CQDs-H2O2 systems. In contrast, a slow and faint CL emission (～400 a.u.) was obtained when injecting N-CQDs into the cell containing K2S2O8 solution (inset of Fig. 3A). Moreover, the CL intensity increased by about 18-fold after the addition of H2O2 to the N-CQDs-K2S2O8 system. Furthermore, the introduction of 0.1 M AA to this system resulted in the inhibition of over 98.6% of the CL intensity. Surprisingly, unlike other commonly observed chemiluminescence systems, the interaction between N-CQDs and K2S2O8 in the presence of H2O2 exhibited a strong and stable CL signal (Fig. 3B). It is worth noting that the signal decayed by 5% of the maximum intensity after 700 s, indicating a prolonged duration and classifying the CL behavior of N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 as glow-type. Moreover, compared to other glow-type CL systems in previous studies (Table S2), the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system belongs to long-lasting CL. The combination of a relatively strong signal and a sluggish emissive decline in CL intensity offers advantages in the term of improving the accuracy and reproducibility of analysis.

To elucidate whether the unique CL property of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system resulted from N-CQDs or its synthetic origin, namely CQDs and N2H4·H2O, we chose CQDs, N2H4·H2O or a combination of both mixed with K2S2O8-H2O2 as the control groups (Fig. 3C). It was found that only the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 brought a considerable signal, while none of the three control groups exhibited any detectable CL intensities, further confirming that CQDs and N2H4·H2O were not responsible for the CL emission and N-CQDs was the resource of the CL signal. The difference in CL behavior between CQDs and N-CQDs may be due to the formation of pyrrole N/pyrazole N groups on the surface of N-CQDs by Knorr reaction.

In addition, the effects of different reagent mixing orders of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system on CL intensity were investigated (Fig. 3D), and three different mixing orders, including K2S2O8 injected into the mixture of N-CQDs and H2O2 (blue curve), N-CQDs injected into the mixture of K2S2O8 and H2O2 (green curve), H2O2 injected into the mixture of N-CQDs and K2S2O8 (orange curve) were compared. It was found that injecting H2O2 at last (orange curve) could get the largest signal, indicating a significant contribution of H2O2 to the enhanced CL. We speculated that H2O2 could produce •OH and O2 •− species and inject electrons and holes to N-CQDs continuously [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL mechanism of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the CL mechanism of this long-lasting CL system, the CL spectra of N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 were studied. It was observed that the CL emission peak of the system was located about 520 nm (Fig. S4), which is consistent with the emission wavelength of the fluorescence spectrum of the N-CQDs when excited at 460 nm, indicating that N-CQDs served as the CL emitter in this system [37].

Electron spin resonance (ESR) is the most direct evidence to identify the generated reactive oxygen species (ROS). In order to explore the presence of •OH and O2 •− during N-CQDs oxidation processes, a radical scavenger known as 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) was employed [38]. The ESR spectra presented a typical peak of DMPO-OH adduct (Fig. 4 A and 4B), and the characteristic signal of DMPO-OOH adduct was also obtained in the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system (Fig. 4C and 4D), thus, confirming that •OH and O2 •− were generated in the CL co-reaction system. 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidine (TEMP) was selected as the trapping agent to identify 1O2 [39]. In addition, the spin adduct 2,2,6,6‐tetramethyl‐1‐piperidinyloxyl (TEMPO) formed by the reaction of TEMP with 1O2, as shown in Fig. 4E and F, provides evidence for the generation of 1O2 in both the N-CQDs-K2S2O8 and N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 CL reaction. Furthermore, distinctly higher ESR signals were seen for DMPO-OH, DMPO-OOH and TEMPO in the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system (Fig. 4, line b) than the N-CQDs-K2S2O8 system (Fig. 4, line a). Hence, the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 CL system can not only produce •OH, O2 •− and 1O2, but also enhance the amount of these three free radicals compared with N-CQDs-K2S2O8 system. We measured the ESR spectra at different reaction intervals (2 min and 5 min) to confirm the long-lasting CL behavior of N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 and verify the ongoing production of ROS. The intensities of the ESR signal for DMPO-OH, DMPO-OOH and TEMPO in the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system noticeably increased as the reaction proceeded, resulting in a glow-type CL phenomenon.

Based on the results of above studies, the possible reaction mechanism for the system was proposed. The long-lasting CL system comprised of N-CQDs as luminophore, and K2S2O8 and H2O2 as dual coreactants. Potassium persulfate is a common oxidant, and under alkaline conditions, it undergoes rapid hydrolysis to form hydroperoxide (HO2 −) (reaction (1). This hydroperoxide subsequently reduces another persulfate molecule, generating sulfate radical (SO4 •−) and sulfate anion (SO4 2−), while being oxidized to superoxide (O2 •−) (reaction (2). Furthermore, in alkaline conditions, SO4 •− reacts with hydroxide to form hydroxyl radical (•OH) (reaction (3) [40].
(1) S2O8 2−+2H2O→2SO4 2−+HO2 −+3H+
(2) S2O8 2−+HO2 −→SO4 2−+SO4 •−+H++O2 •−
(3) SO4 •−+OH−→SO4 2−+•OH

The radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and electrons in the carbon dots accounts for the CL emission [41]. Therefore, it can be assumed that the base-catalyzed hydrolysis of persulfate produces reactive oxygen radicals (•OH and O2 •−) and facilitates the electron and hole injection to N-CQDs, leading to an increase in the amounts of N-CQDs•− and N-CQDs•+. Then, the annihilation of N-CQDs•− and N-CQDs•+ gives rise to the formation of N-CQDs*, which return to the ground state by photon emission (reaction (4)–(7).
(4) N-CQDs + O2 •−→N-CQDs•−+O2
(5) N-CQDs+•OH + H+→N-CQDs•++H2O
(6) N-CQDs•++N-CQDs•−→N-CQDs*+N-CQDs
(7) N-CQDs*→N-CQDs + hv

In addition, the N-CQDs-K2S2O8 system shows considerable CL emission upon the addition of H2O2, because H2O2 can produce •OH and O2 •− under neutral and alkaline conditions (reaction (8)–(9) [42].
(8) H2O2 + OH−→HO2 −•+H2O
(9) H2O2 + HO2 −•→•OH + O2 •−+H2O

Furthermore, •OH and O2 •− can engage in Fenton-like reactions, producing CL originating from the dimol singlet-oxygen (1O2)2* (reaction (10)–(11). This excited-state dimmer is formed through the interaction of singlet oxygen and emits in a broad wavelength range between 400 to 650 nm, which overlaps the excitation wavelength for N-CQDs [43]. Thus, (1O2)2* may act as an CL energy donor and transfer its energy to N-CQDs via chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET). Consequently, this process yields the excited-state N-CQDs (N-CQDs*) which subsequently return to their ground states with light emission (reaction (12)–(13). In summary, a possible CL mechanism involves three key processes. Firstly, base-activated persulfate could persistently produce reactive species (•OH and O2 •−), thereby extending the CL duration time. Then, the generated reactive species inject electrons and holes into N-CQDs, resulting in N-CQDs•− and N-CQDs•+, respectively. The annihilation of N-CQDs•− and N-CQDs•+ results in the formation of N-CQDs* and CL emission. Finally, the addition of H2O2 to the system enhances CL emission by generating additional ROS under neutral and alkaline conditions.
(10) •OH + O2 •−→1O2 + OH−
(11)
1O2+1O2→(1O2)2*
(12) (1O2)2*+N-CQDs→N-CQDs*+2O2
(13) N-CQDs*→N-CQDs + hv

Some experimental parameters affect the intensity and duration of CL emission. The reaction conditions were optimized for the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 CL system. As shown in Fig. S5A, the CL reaction generally occurs in basic media. We examined the CL intensities under different pH (from 2 to 13) condition, and the system produced the strongest CL at pH 12 media. However, considering that AA is easily decomposed under alkaline conditions, a pH of 7.4 was selected as the optimal condition for AA detection in subsequent experiments. The CL emission was seriously affected by N-CQDs concentration with a range of 5ⅹ10−4 to 5ⅹ102 μg/mL. The maximum CL intensity is observed at a concentration of 5 μg/mL of N-CQDs (Fig. S5B), thus establishing it as the optimal value. The influence of K2S2O8 and H2O2 concentrations was studied within the range of 0.06 − 0.14 M and 5 − 5ⅹ105 μM, respectively (Fig. S5C and Fig. S5D). The CL intensity increased with increasing K2S2O8 or H2O2 concentration. However, a deceleration in signal enhancement was observed when the K2S2O8 concentration reached 0.1 M. Consequently, 0.1 M of K2S2O8 was chosen for further investigations, while the appropriate concentration of H2O2 was determined to be 5ⅹ105 μM. We explored the effect of incubation time on the AA detection in the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system (Fig. S5E). After adding 10 mM AA for two minutes, the CL signal tends to be stable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 based CL sensor for AA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical merit of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 based CL sensor for the detection of AA at different concentrations was assessed under optimized conditions. Fig. 5 A demonstrates that the relative intensity of CL, ΔI CL (ΔI CL = I 0 – I, I 0 and I are the relative CL intensity in the absence and presence of AA, respectively) exhibited a linear response to the logarithm of AA concentration in the range of 0.1 to 10.0 mM, with a high correlation coefficient of 0.9974. The regression equation was expressed as ΔI CL = 2184.66 lg C AA (mM) + 2335.80. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.036 mM, corresponding to a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Compared to other methods for AA determination, this method is also suitable for the detection of vitamin C tablets (Table S3). In addition, this CL sensor for the detection of AA showed good stability within 15 days (Fig.S6).

The anti-interference performance of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 based CL sensing platform for AA determination was also evaluated. The CL responses of a series of sample solutions containing AA (1 mM) and various interfering substances (100 mM), including common ions (Zn2+, Ca2+ and Cu2+), excipients commonly found in tablets (starch, sucrose, citric acid and magnesium stearate) and other antioxidants (vitamin E, melatonin, lipoic acid and folic acid), were researched. It was observed that all these interfering components had little or no effect on AA detection (Fig. 5B), demonstrating that our method possesses excellent specificity.

The CL assay was applied to detect AA in actual samples of vitamin C tablets. To evaluate the accuracy of the method, recovery tests were conducted, and the results were presented in Table 2 . It is worth noting that the recovery ranged between 95.3% and 100%, with the relative standard deviation (RSD) ranging from 3.5% to 4.5%, indicating the reliability of the present method for AA determination in real samples.

In summary, a glow-type CL has been constructed based on the novel synthesized N-CQDs, and was observed to be able to generate weak and persistent CL when exposed to K2S2O8. The injection of H2O2 resulted in an amplifying effect on the N-CQDs-K2S2O8 CL system and the CL emission decayed by 5 % of the maximum intensity over 700 s. Furthermore, the mechanisms of the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 CL system was attributed to the concurrence of K2S2O8 and H2O2 that leads to continuously generate •OH, O2 •− and 1O2. Finally, the N-CQDs-K2S2O8-H2O2 system-based CL assay sensing platform in detecting AA. This method has been applied to the analysis of tablet samples and have the potential to create novel avenues for CL visual sensing and imagines.
This research did not involve human or animal samples.

Shu-Hua Han: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Dan-Dan Huang: Investigation, Data curation. Zhang-Jian Cheng: Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ai-Lin Liu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization. Yun Lei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82373838 & 82072379), Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (2020Y9009), and the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (2023 J01551).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124730.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
